title: Kettle stitch
subtitle: The link stitch at head and tail that ties signatures together
excerpt: The kettle stitch is a small linking stitch worked at the head and tail sewing stations after the thread exits each successive signature. Without it, multi-signature bindings unravel from the ends. It takes two minutes to learn and a few hours of practice to make consistently tidy.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on sewing, the kettle stitch worked between successive signatures at head and tail. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

content.text: holds every multi-signature sewn binding together. After the thread exits the last
, the stitch loops under the thread that links the previous two signatures and re-enters the new signature. It is worked at the
stations of every signature from the second onward.

items.qty: Made from scrap paper, with head and tail stations pierced at 8 mm from the fold ends
items.name: Two pre-sewn practice signatures

attrs.heading: Tools for practising the kettle stitch

content.text: Sew through the text block to the head station of the current signature. Exit the signature from inside to outside at the head sewing station.
Locate the linking thread between the previous two signatures at the same head station. This thread runs along the spine between two exit holes one signature back.
Slide the needle under that linking thread, from the head toward the tail (into the gap). The needle passes through the loop without piercing any paper.
Pull the thread through until snug. Do not overtighten: snug closes the loop; tight cuts into the paper at the station.
Re-enter the current signature at the same head station, passing from outside to inside. The thread forms a small knot-like loop at the exit point. Proceed across the rest of the stations.
Repeat the same sequence at the tail station of the same signature. Each signature gets a kettle stitch at both head and tail.

attrs.intro: Problems that arise when first practising the kettle stitch.

items.fix: Work in good raking light. Run a fingernail along the spine to lift the thread slightly off the surface before sliding the needle under.
items.cause: On the first two signatures, there is only one linking thread, not two. Look for the single thread running between the exit holes of signatures 1 and 2.
items.symptom: The linking thread is hard to find on the spine

items.fix: Draw the thread through until the loop closes against the spine, then hold it lightly with a fingertip while re-entering the station.
items.cause: Thread was not drawn snug before re-entering, leaving slack in the loop.
items.symptom: The kettle stitch pulls loose after re-entering the signature

items.fix: Use a blunt-tip bookbinding needle. Approach the linking thread from the side, sliding the needle horizontally under the thread rather than piercing down through it.
items.cause: The needle tip is too sharp, or the entry angle was too steep.
items.symptom: The needle splits the linking thread rather than passing under it

attrs.heading: Common problems

content.text: The kettle stitch is used in every multi-signature sewn binding in this collection.
